elements.sent-2411-001: In decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning, agents learning in isolation can lead to relative over-generalization (RO), where optimal joint actions are undervalued in favor of suboptimal ones.
elements.sent-2411-002: This hinders effective coordination in cooperative tasks, as agents tend to choose actions that are individually rational but collectively suboptimal.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address this issue, we introduce MaxMax Q-Learning (MMQ), which employs an iterative process of sampling and evaluating potential next states, selecting those with maximal Q-values for learning.
elements.sent-2411-004: This approach refines approximations of ideal state transitions, aligning more closely with the optimal joint policy of collaborating agents.
elements.sent-2411-005: We provide theoretical analysis supporting MMQ's potential and present empirical evaluations across various environments susceptible to RO.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our results demonstrate that MMQ frequently outperforms existing baselines, exhibiting enhanced convergence and sample efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-007: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) has become increasingly important for addressing complex real-world challenges that require coordinated behaviors among multiple agents.
elements.sent-2411-008: Successful applications included playing card games \citep{Brown2018Superhuman , autonomous driving \citep{shalev2016safe, pmlr-v155-zhou21a , unmanned aerial vehicles \citep{wang2020multi , wireless sensor networks \citep{xu2020learningnetworks, sahraoui2021schedule and traffic light control \citep{bazzan2009opportunitiescontrol, zhou2023novel .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: A dominant framework in MARL is centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) \citep{lowe2017multi,2018RashidQMIX,son2019qtran , which often relies on a centralized coordinator to aggregate information, such as local observation or individual actions from other agents during training.}
elements.sent-2411-010: While this approach has been widely adopted due to its effectiveness in leveraging global information, it may still face scalability challenges, particularly in environments with large number of agents or complex interactions.
elements.sent-2411-011: Additionally, in scenarios where privacy is a critical concern, CTDE methods that require access to individual agent data could be less desirable.
elements.sent-2411-012: While many CTDE methods do not require exhaustive local information from all agents, the potential for scalability and privacy issues in certain implementations warrants consideration.
elements.sent-2411-013: While inter-agent communication can partially mitigate some of these challenges \citep{foerster2016learning, zhu2022surveycommunication , it introduces additional overhead, which can be prohibitive in environments where communication is costly or unreliable.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Consider large-scale drone swarms for search and rescue or surveillance tasks \citep{baldazo2019decentralized_drone, batra2022decentralized_swarms .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In a CTDE framework, centrally aggregating data from each drone can be impractical due to network delays, dynamic environments, and potential communication failures.
elements.sent-2411-016: Additionally, communication frameworks may struggle with channel congestion or high packet loss, especially in complex terrain or with electronic interference.
elements.sent-2411-017: Fully decentralized learning presents a promising alternative, where agents rely solely on their experiences without considering the actions or policies of other agents during both training and execution.
elements.sent-2411-018: This approach enables the system to scale effectively and remain resilient to communication issues.
elements.sent-2411-019: However, decentralized approaches come with their own challenges.
elements.sent-2411-020: From an individual agent's perspective, the learning process occurs within a non-stationary MDP, as the transition probabilities change due to the evolving policies of other agents.
elements.sent-2411-021: Existing decentralized MARL methods, including optimism strategies in Q-learning \citep{lauer2000algorithm, matignon2007hysteretic, wei2016lenient , attempt to mitigate these challenges.}
elements.sent-2411-022: More recently, Ideal Independent Q-learning (I2Q) \citep{jiang2022i2q explicitly models state transitions assuming optimal joint behavior, introducing ideal transition probabilities to address non-stationarity in independent Q-learning.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Another critical issue in decentralised MARL is Relative Over-generalisation (RO), where agents prefer suboptimal policies because individual actions appear preferable without coordinated strategies.
elements.sent-2411-024: First defined and studied within the field of cooperative co-evolutionary algorithms \citep{wiegand2004analysis, panait2006biasing, panait2007analysis , it has more recently been explored predominantly in centralised MARL contexts \citep{rashid2020weighted, gupta2021uneven, shi2022curriculum .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: RO occurs when agents adapt their actions to limited interactions, often focusing on the exploratory behaviours of other agents.
elements.sent-2411-026: Consequently, agents may favour more robust but less optimal solutions in the absence of coordinated strategies.
elements.sent-2411-027: In decentralised learning settings, this problem has been discussed within simple matrix game scenarios \citep{wei2016lenient .}
elements.sent-2411-028: RO, exacerbated by non-stationarity, presents a significant challenge as agents make decisions based on fluctuating global rewards without the benefit of coordinated strategies \citep{matignon2012independent, wei2016lenient .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Although previous implementations of optimistic strategies have shown some efficacy, our empirical results indicate they fall short in cooperative tasks with pronounced RO challenges.
elements.sent-2411-030: This paper introduces MaxMax Q-Learning (MMQ), a novel algorithm designed to address the RO problem in decentralised MARL settings.
elements.sent-2411-031: MMQ aims to mitigate the challenges posed by RO and non-stationarity in decentralised learning environments.
elements.sent-2411-032: The key insight behind MMQ is enabling agents to reason about beneficial experiences that occur infrequently.
elements.sent-2411-033: At its core, MMQ employs two non-parameterised quantile models to capture the range of state transitions, accounting for both environmental factors and the evolving policies of learning agents.
elements.sent-2411-034: These models iteratively sample, evaluate, and select optimal states, refining the approximation of ideal state transitions and facilitating global reward maximisation.
elements.sent-2411-035: The state with the highest Q-value is then selected to update the value function, promoting convergence towards optimal Q-values in the context of other agents' best actions.
elements.sent-2411-036: The MMQ algorithm incorporates two maximum operators in the Bellman update: the first takes the maximum over all possible next states to select the most promising future scenario, and the second takes the maximum over Q-values of state-action pairs to determine the best action in that scenario.
elements.sent-2411-037: MMQ's adaptive nature, which involves continuously updating the ranges of possible next states, enables effective decision-making in dynamic environments.
elements.sent-2411-038: The main contributions of this paper are threefold.
elements.sent-2411-039: First, we introduce MMQ, a novel algorithm that employs quantile models to capture multi-agent dynamics and approximate ideal state transitions through sampling.
elements.sent-2411-040: Second, we provide a theoretical demonstration of MMQ's potential to converge to globally optimal joint policies, assuming perfect knowledge of forward and value functions.
elements.sent-2411-041: Third, we present empirical results showing that MMQ often outperforms or matches established baselines across various cooperative tasks, highlighting its potential for faster convergence, enhanced sample efficiency, and improved reward maximisation in decentralised learning environments.
elements.sent-2411-042: The remainder of this paper is structured as follows: Section 2 discusses related work in MARL and uncertainty quantification.
elements.sent-2411-043: Section 3 provides background on multi-agent Markov Decision Processes and the challenges of relative over-generalization.
elements.sent-2411-044: Section 4 presents the methodology of MaxMax Q-Learning, including its theoretical foundations and implementation details.
elements.sent-2411-045: Section 5 describes our experimental setup and results across various environments.
elements.sent-2411-046: Finally, Section 6 concludes the paper with a discussion of our findings and potential directions for future research.

elements.sent-2411-068: Quantifying different sources of uncertainty is crucial in reinforcement learning, particularly in multi-agent settings.

elements.sent-2411-073: Our proposed MMQ algorithm addresses these challenges by using quantile networks to effectively manage two key sources of epistemic uncertainty in multi-agent settings: limited experiential data and evolving strategies of other agents.

elements.sent-2411-132: Our combined learning and sampling approach facilitates the gradual convergence of the agents' policies toward the globally-optimal joint policy.
elements.sent-2411-133: This gradual convergence is supported by the insights from the following theorem, which shows the disparity between the optimal Q-values and those learned by the agents is limited by the difference between the best next state in the estimated set and the true best next state.
elements.sent-2411-134: This bounding relationship is crucial, as it indicates that the closer our estimated set of next states is to the actual set composed of all the possible next states, the more accurate the estimations of the agents' optimal Q-functions become.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{MMQ for each agent $i$} \label{pseudocode_h} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} Q-network $Q_i$, actor network $\pi_i$ and target networks $\overline{Q}_i$, $\overline{\pi}_i$; Quantile models $g^{\tau_l}_i$ and $g^{\tau_u}_i$; Reward network $R_i$; Replay buffer $D_i$ \FOR{$t=1,\dots,T_{\mathrm{max}}$} \STATE All agents interact with the environment using random action (for initial $P$ steps) or $\varepsilon$-greedy policy and store experiences $(s,a_i,r,s')$ in $D_i$ \STATE Sample a mini-batch from $\mathcal{D}_i$; \STATE Update $g^{\tau_l}_i$ and $g^{\tau_u}_i$ by minimizing \eqref{eq:Quan_loss} \STATE Draw $M$ samples from predicted bound $[g_i^{\tau_l}(\cdot), g_i^{\tau_u}(\cdot)]$ to construct set $\hat{\mathcal{S}}$ \STATE Calculate the target value over $\hat{\mathcal{S}}$ using \eqref{eq:target} \STATE Update $Q_i$ multiple times by minimizing \eqref{eq:Q_loss} \STATE Update $R_i$ by minimizing \eqref{eq:R_loss} \STATE Update $\pi_i$ by minimizing \eqref{eq:A_loss} \STATE Update the target network $\overline{Q}_i$ and $\overline{\pi}_i$ \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-164: To capture the range of possible next states, our implementation utilises two non-parametrised quantile models, \( g^{\tau_l _i \) and \( g^{\tau_u _i \), which employ neural networks to predict the \( \tau_l = 0.05 \) and \( \tau_u = 0.95 \) quantiles for each dimension of the next state.}}
elements.sent-2411-165: The neural network parameters, denoted by \( \phi^l_i \) and \( \phi^u_i \), are learnt by minimising the quantile loss according to their respective \( \tau \) values over samples from individual replay buffer $D_i$: \begin{equation}L(\phi_i) = \mathbb{E}_{s,a_i \sim \mathcal{D}_i}[L^{\tau}(g_i^{\tau}(s, a_i; \phi_i) - s')], \label{eq:Quan_loss}\end{equation} where \( L^{\tau (u) = \mathbb{I (u > 0) \tau u + \mathbb{I (u < 0) (1 - \tau) u \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-166: For each \( (s, a_i) \) pair, the two quantile models predict bounds \([g_i^{\tau_l (s, a_i), g_i^{\tau_u (s, a_i)]\).}}
elements.sent-2411-167: We then construct the potential next state set \( \hat{\mathcal{S \) by including the true \( s' \) and \( M \) samples drawn from the quantile bounds.}}
elements.sent-2411-168: We also explored a parametrised multivariate Gaussian model as another method to estimate the possible next states, detailed in Appendix \ref{Quantile_Gau .}
elements.sent-2411-169: Each agent also learns a Q-network, parameterised by \( \theta_i \), and represented as \( Q_i(s, a_i; \theta_i) \).
elements.sent-2411-170: The target value for the Q-network update is given by: \begin{equation}Y_i(s, a_i; \theta_i) = \max_{\hat{s}' \in \hat{\mathcal{S}}} \left( R_i(s, s'; \psi_i) + \gamma \max_{a'_i} Q_i(\hat{s}', a'_i; \theta_i) \right), \label{eq:target}\end{equation} where \( R_i(s, s'; \psi_i) \) is an estimate from a learned reward model parameterised by \( \psi_i \).
elements.sent-2411-171: Here we apply a stop-gradient operator to the target value to prevent gradient flow back to the next state estimation process.
elements.sent-2411-172: This operation is crucial for maintaining stability in the learning dynamics by separating the optimization of the Q-network from the updates to the next state estimation.
elements.sent-2411-173: The loss function for optimising the Q-network parameter \( \theta_i \) is then given by \begin{equation}L(\theta_i) = \mathbb{E}_{s,a_i\sim \mathcal{D}_i}\left[ Q_i(s, a_i; \theta_i) - Y_i(s, a_i; \theta_i) \right]^2. \label{eq:Q_loss}\end{equation} This loss aims to align the Q-network's predictions with the maximum expected return, considering both the immediate reward and the discounted future Q-values of the potential next states sampled from the estimated quantile bound.
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}L(\theta_i) = E_{s,a_i\sim D_i}\left[ Q_i(s, a_i; \theta_i) - Y_i(s, a_i; \theta_i) \right]^2. \label{eq:Q_loss}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-174: Additionally, the learned reward function \( R_i(s, s'; \psi_i) \), parameterised by \( \psi_i \), is trained to approximate the rewards for state transitions.
elements.sent-2411-175: The loss for this reward model is the mean squared error between the predicted and actual rewards, \begin{equation}L(\psi_i) = \mathbb{E}_{s\sim\mathcal{D}_i}[R_i(s, s'; \psi_i) - r]^2. \label{eq:R_loss}\end{equation} To deal with continuous action spaces, each agent uses an actor network \( \pi_i(s; \rho_i) \), parameterised by \( \rho_i \), to learn its policy.
elements.sent-2411-176: The loss function for the actor network, aimed at maximising the Q-value, is \begin{equation}L(\rho_i) = \mathbb{E}_{s\sim\mathcal{D}_i}[-Q_i(s, \pi_i(s; \rho_i); \theta_i)]. \label{eq:A_loss}\end{equation} The training process is summarised in Algorithm \ref{pseudocode_h , and the full source code is available at \href{https://github.com/Tingz0/Maxmax\_Q\_learning {https://github.com/Tingz0/Maxmax\_Q\_learning .}}}
elements.sent-2411-177: The full algorithm interleaves the optimisation of various constituent models, allowing agents to adaptively learn and improve their policies based on their own experiences and the evolving environmental dynamics.

elements.sent-2411-180: Second, negative reward shifting \citep{sun2022exploit , which enhances our double-max-style updates (see also Appendix \ref{app:negative ).}}

elements.sent-2411-259: In this work, we introduced MaxMax Q-learning (MMQ), a novel algorithm designed to mitigate the Relative Over-generalisation problem in multi-agent collaborative tasks through the use of quantile models and optimistic sampling.
elements.sent-2411-260: Our key theoretical contribution establishes a connection between the accuracy of estimating the best next state and the convergence towards globally optimal joint policies within the MMQ framework, providing a solid foundation for understanding the algorithm's behaviour and performance.
elements.sent-2411-261: Empirically, we demonstrated the effectiveness of MMQ across three diverse tasks, showing its ability to outperform existing baselines in terms of convergence speed, sample efficiency, and final performance.
elements.sent-2411-262: A notable outcome is MMQ’s scalability, as it can accommodate an increased number of agents while achieving superior performance with minimal samples, which reduces computational overhead—a crucial factor in practical multi-agent systems.
elements.sent-2411-263: Despite these promising results, several limitations of MMQ remain.
elements.sent-2411-264: First, the Monte Carlo optimisation error in MMQ may exhibit exponential dependence as the dimensionality of the state space increases.
elements.sent-2411-265: However, this issue is somewhat mitigated in multi-agent settings, where the use of partially-observed MDPs limits the effective dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-266: Second, although MMQ has demonstrated scalability in our experiments, challenges may arise in environments with very large populations of agents, where increased computational costs and coordination complexity could impact performance.
elements.sent-2411-267: Looking ahead, future work may aim to address these limitations and further enhance MMQ’s capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-268: One promising direction involves developing adaptive mechanisms to gauge the informativeness of observations about other agents, which could improve the robustness of reward function learning, especially in scenarios with partial or noisy observations.
elements.sent-2411-269: Additionally, relaxing the current assumption of independence across dimensions in the quantile model by predicting the covariance matrix could capture more complex state dynamics, potentially leading to more accurate estimations.
elements.sent-2411-270: Further improvements in scalability might be achieved by reducing the number of samples required as the state space grows, thereby lowering computational demands in high-dimensional and large-agent scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-271: Together, these future developments aim to overcome current limitations and extend the applicability of MMQ to a broader range of multi-agent scenarios.

elements.sent-2411-318: To enhance exploration and mitigate overestimation in our MMQ approach, we have integrated a negative linear reward shifting technique \citep{sun2022exploit .}
elements.sent-2411-319: This method involves a downward adjustment of the reward function (by subtracting a constant from all rewards) during training.
elements.sent-2411-320: Analogous to starting with a higher initial value in the neural value function, this adjustment assigns inflated Q-values to less frequently visited state-action pairs, thus prioritizing them during the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-321: Our implementation of this insight prioritizes optimal next states, which are initially less common than suboptimal ones.
elements.sent-2411-322: By initializing the value function at a higher level, these optimal states receive more favorable evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-323: This technique proves particularly beneficial in our double-max-style updates, as it aids in the accurate selection of next states for updates and counteracts the overestimation of Q-values associated with chosen actions.
elements.sent-2411-324: We conducted an ablation study on negative reward shifting in the differential games with \(N=2\) and \(N=4\), depicted in Figure \ref{fig:neg .}
elements.sent-2411-325: The results indicated that negative reward shifting significantly enhanced the performance of our algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-326: Additionally, we visualized the evolution of the learned Q-values during the initial 16 updates of training in Figure \ref{fig:Q_NC .}
elements.sent-2411-327: With the same Q-value initialization, the central part of the state space, which represents infrequently achieved states, exhibited higher values than the edge parts under the negative reward shifting regimen.
elements.sent-2411-328: In contrast, when utilizing the standard positive reward setting, the central part displayed lower values compared to the edges initially, underscoring the effectiveness of this reward adjustment strategy in promoting a more balanced exploration and accurate value estimation.

elements.sent-2411-329: In the MMQ algorithm, each agent uses two quantile models to capture variations in environment dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-330: While a single model on each side of the quantile bound provides valuable insights, its capacity to fully encapsulate the range of possible outcomes for state-action pairs can be limited.
elements.sent-2411-331: This limitation stems from the evolving nature of the agents' policies and the inherent uncertainties within the environment.
elements.sent-2411-332: To enhance the robustness of our model and better address these challenges, we experimented with an ensemble approach.
elements.sent-2411-333: We utilized \( K \) pairs of models, denoted as \( \{g^{\tau_l _{i,k (s' | s, a_i; \phi^l_{k,i )\}_{k=1 ^K \) and \( \{g^{\tau_u _{i,k (s' | s, a_i; \phi^u_{k,i )\}_{k=1 ^K \), where each model in the ensemble is characterized by its own unique parameter set \( \phi_{k,i \).}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-334: This ensemble configuration allows for a broader representation of the dynamics, providing a more diverse set of potential outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-335: We define \( \mathcal{S ^*_{s, a_i \) as the union of outcomes from all models: \( \mathcal{S ^*_{s, a_i = \bigcup_{k=1 ^K \{s'_{k, 1 ,\dots, s'_{k, M}\} \), where \( s'_{k, 1 ,\dots, s'_{k, M \) are independent and identically distributed samples drawn from the bounds predicted by each pair of quantile models.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-336: During the initial learning phases, these \( K \) models yield a broader range of predictions, as depicted in Fig.\ref{fig:ablaE_MeanDim , reflecting different potential outcomes for the same state-action pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-337: However, this approach did not lead to further improvement in performance, as a single quantile network was already sufficient to predict the bounds accurately.
elements.sent-2411-338: Consequently, this ensemble method did not enhance our algorithm's effectiveness in practice.

elements.subsection-2411-013: Comparison between quantile model and Gaussian model
elements.sent-2411-339: For the Gaussian model, the neural network parameters, are learned by minimizing a loss function based on the negative log-likelihood of the Gaussian distribution using experiences from its replay buffer $\mathcal{D _i$: \begin{equation}L(\phi_i) = -E_{s,a_i\simD_i}\left[ \sum_{d=1}^D \log \left( N(s'_d | \mu_{\phi_i, d}(s, a_i), \sigma_{\phi_i, d}^2(s, a_i)) \right)\right]. \label{eq:F_loss}\end{equation} Here, \( \mu_{\phi_i, d (s, a_i) \) and \( \sigma_{\phi_i, d ^2(s, a_i) \) are the neural network's predictions of the mean and variance for each dimension \(d\) of the next state \(s'\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-340: The loss function sums the log-likelihoods across all dimensions \(D\) of the state space, focusing on accurately modeling the distribution of each state dimension.
elements.sent-2411-341: As shown in \ref{fig:pred_var , the Gaussian model could also capture the other agents' influence on the observed state changes.}
elements.sent-2411-342: The result in \ref{fig:com_G_Q showed MMQ with the quantile model is slightly better than MMQ with the Gaussian model.}

elements.sent-2411-343: To assess the robustness of our algorithm in the stochastic environment, we add Gaussian noise to the transition and the reward function.
elements.sent-2411-344: For stochastic state transition, we add a Gaussian noise to the position update of each agent: $x_i=\clip\{x_i + 0.1 \times a_i + z, -1, 1\}$, while $z\sim\mathcal{N (0,\sigma^2)$.}
elements.sent-2411-345: Here we test in two levels of stochasticity: $\sigma_s=0.02$ and $\sigma_s=0.05$.
elements.sent-2411-346: For the stochastic reward, we add Gaussian noise to the reward, $z\sim\mathcal{N (0,\sigma^2)$, and the reward becomes $r = r + z$.}
elements.sent-2411-347: We also test in two levels of noise: $\sigma_r=0.05$ and $\sigma_r=0.1$.
elements.sent-2411-348: In Figure \ref{fig:DG_noisy , with noise in state transition, our algorithm still perform the best compared to other baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-349: Although we use a deterministic function as reward function, our algorithm still find the global optimum for all seeds when there exits reward noise whereas the performance of other baselines degraded.
